question:
Floyd-Warshall is classically used for:
A. Single-source shortest paths B. All-pairs shortest paths C. Minimum spanning tree D. Maximum flow E. I am not sure

Standard Floyd-Warshall time complexity is:
A. O(V^2) B. O(E log V) C. O(V^3) D. O(VE) E. I am not sure

Standard Floyd-Warshall space complexity is:
A. O(V) B. O(V^2) C. O(E) D. O(V^3) E. I am not sure

Standard Floyd-Warshall recurrence update is:
A. d[i][j]=min(d[i][j], d[i][k]+d[k][j]) B. d[i][j]=max(d[i][j], d[i][k]+d[k][j]) C. d[i][j]=d[i][k]*d[k][j] D. d[i][j]=d[i][j]+d[k][k] E. I am not sure

With 0-based outer loop k, after finishing iteration k, allowed intermediate vertices are:
A. {0,1,...,k} B. {k,k+1,...,n-1} C. none (direct edges only) D. all vertices from the beginning E. I am not sure

Floyd-Warshall on negative edges is:
A. Invalid always B. Valid if no negative cycles C. Valid only on DAGs D. Equivalent to Prim E. I am not sure

A negative cycle is detected by Floyd-Warshall iff:
A. there exists i with d[i][i] < 0 B. there exists i,j with d[i][j] < 0 C. sum of diagonal entries is positive D. matrix is asymmetric E. I am not sure

In in-place Floyd-Warshall, the required outermost loop variable is usually:
A. i B. j C. k D. Any order is equivalent E. I am not sure

After finishing outer-loop value k in Floyd-Warshall, dist[i][j] allows intermediates from:
A. Vertices in set {1..k} B. Only vertex k C. Only source and destination D. Vertices with degree <= k E. I am not sure

To reconstruct actual shortest paths, Floyd-Warshall often keeps:
A. next/predecessor matrix B. union-find parent only C. stack of DFS states D. degree histogram E. I am not sure

Floyd-Warshall naturally supports:
A. Directed and undirected weighted graphs B. Only unweighted graphs C. Only trees D. Only complete DAGs E. I am not sure

With V vertices, standard Floyd-Warshall worst-case time complexity is:
A. O(V^3) B. O(VE) C. O((V+E)logV) D. O(V^2) E. I am not sure

Compared to repeated Dijkstra, Floyd-Warshall is often attractive for:
A. Very sparse huge graphs B. Dense graphs or many all-pairs queries C. Single-source one-shot queries only D. Only negative-cycle graphs E. I am not sure

For in-place Floyd-Warshall correctness, the outermost loop variable must be:
A. source i B. target j C. intermediate k D. edge index e E. I am not sure

To reconstruct actual shortest paths in Floyd-Warshall, a common extra structure is:
A. Union-find parent array B. next/predecessor matrix C. In-degree array D. DFS color array E. I am not sure

Floyd-Warshall handles zero-weight edges:
A. Incorrectly B. Only in undirected graphs C. Only with integer weights D. Correctly E. I am not sure

Warshall transitive-closure version replaces min/+ with:
A. OR and AND B. AND and OR C. XOR and OR D. OR and XOR E. I am not sure

After preprocessing, answering one distance query dist[u][v] is:
A. O(V) B. O(log V) C. O(1) D. O(E) E. I am not sure

After Floyd-Warshall preprocessing, answering one distance query dist[u][v] is:
A. O(1) B. O(log V) C. O(V) D. O(E) E. I am not sure

Floyd-Warshall can be interpreted as DP over:
A. Edge sorting order B. Allowed intermediate vertex set C. DFS recursion depth only D. Node colors E. I am not sure

Floyd-Warshall is NOT an algorithm for:
A. All-pairs shortest paths B. Negative-cycle indication via diagonal C. Minimum spanning tree construction D. Dense-graph APSP preprocessing E. I am not sure

Floyd-Warshall is NOT directly for:
A. Max-flow computation B. APSP with possible negative edges C. Reachability closure variant D. Path reconstruction with next matrix E. I am not sure

With all nonnegative edges, Floyd-Warshall is:
A. Incorrect B. Still correct C. Correct only for odd V D. Equivalent to BFS exactly E. I am not sure

In Floyd-Warshall, candidate path via k combines two subpaths that each use intermediates:
A. from all vertices without restriction B. only vertices with index greater than k C. only vertices with index at most k-1 D. no intermediates (direct edges only) E. I am not sure

The classic algorithm naming credits are mainly:
A. Floyd and Warshall B. Dijkstra and Prim C. Bellman and Ford D. Knuth and Pratt E. I am not sure
How do you solve the shortest path problem?
In practice, is there any shortest path algorithm based on dynamic programming?
How do you compute all-pairs shortest paths in a weighted graph without negative cycles?
In practice, what algorithm finds the shortest distance between every pair of vertices in a graph?
In practice, is there a dynamic programming approach to solve the shortest path problem for all vertex pairs simultaneously?
In practice, which algorithm uses a three-nested-loop structure to iteratively improve distance estimates between all nodes?
How would you find the shortest path between every two nodes in a graph with O(V³) time complexity?
In practice, what method solves the all-pairs shortest path problem by considering intermediate vertices?
In practice, which algorithm initializes a distance matrix with direct edge weights and then updates it through intermediate nodes?
What algorithm would you use to solve shortest paths for all pairs using a progressively expanding set of allowed intermediate vertices?
In practice, is there an algorithm that works for both directed and undirected graphs to find all-pair shortest paths?
In practice, what approach uses the recurrence relation: dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k] + dist[k][j])?
In practice, which shortest path algorithm is based on the principle of optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems for all pairs?
How would you compute transitive closure with weights, i.e., the shortest path between every pair of vertices?
In practice, what algorithm would you use to find shortest paths between all cities in a transportation network?
